name: Alabama
conStatuteTitle: Alabama Statewide Health Coordinating Council Act
adminAgency: Alabama State Health Planning and Development Agency (SHPDA)
marketConcentration: High concentration in major metro areas. UAB Health System dominates Birmingham. Huntsville Hospital System controls northern Alabama markets.
summary: Alabama maintains one of the most comprehensive CON programs in the Southeast, covering hospitals, ASCs, and imaging. UAB Health System's dominance in Birmingham creates significant market concentration, with prices reaching 289% of Medicare rates.

citations.text: Alabama Certificate of Need Program, Ala. Code § 22-21-260 et seq.
SHPDA Annual Report on CON Applications, 2024
FTC Staff Comment to Alabama Legislature on CON Reform, 2016
Mercatus Center, Certificate-of-Need Laws: Alabama Profile, 2020
CMS Hospital Cost Report Data, UAB Hospital, FY 2023
AHA Annual Survey, Alabama Hospital Market Share, 2023

name: Alaska
marketConcentration: Providence Alaska Medical Center dominates Anchorage. Geographic isolation creates natural monopolies across the state.
summary: Alaska's CON program covers hospitals and ASCs but not imaging. Geographic isolation combined with CON restrictions creates extreme market concentration, with Providence Health dominating the Anchorage market and prices exceeding 312% of Medicare.

citations.text: Alaska Certificate of Need Program, Alaska Stat. § 18.07.031 et seq.
Alaska Department of Health CON Review Process, 2024
Providence Alaska Medical Center Financial Data, CMS Cost Report FY 2023
Mercatus Center, Certificate-of-Need Laws: Alaska Profile, 2020
FTC & DOJ Joint Statement on CON Laws, 2017
AHA Annual Survey, Alaska Hospital Market Data, 2023

name: Connecticut
marketConcentration: Yale New Haven Health System controls over 40% of inpatient market. Hartford HealthCare consolidation accelerating. Two systems control majority of state capacity.
summary: Connecticut's CON program is among the most comprehensive in New England, covering all major facility types. Yale New Haven Health and Hartford HealthCare dominate the market, with prices reaching 305% of Medicare. Recent reforms have raised thresholds but maintained core restrictions.

citations.text: Connecticut Certificate of Need Act, Conn. Gen. Stat. § 19a-638 et seq.
Office of Health Strategy, CON Determination Data, 2024
Yale New Haven Health System Community Health Needs Assessment, 2023
Connecticut Hospital Association Market Report, 2023
FTC Staff Comment on Connecticut CON Modernization, 2019
Mercatus Center, Certificate-of-Need Laws: Connecticut Profile, 2020

name: Delaware
conStatuteCitation: Del. Code Ann. tit. 16, § 9301 et seq.
marketConcentration: ChristianaCare dominates the state with two major hospitals. Bayhealth Medical Center serves southern Delaware.
summary: Delaware maintains a limited CON program covering only hospital construction and expansion. ChristianaCare dominates the state's healthcare market. Detailed market analysis is pending.

citations.text: Delaware Health Resources Management Act, Del. Code Ann. tit. 16, § 9301 et seq.

name: District of Columbia
adminAgency: DC State Health Planning and Development Agency
marketConcentration: MedStar Health operates multiple facilities. George Washington University Hospital and Children's National are key players in a geographically constrained market.
summary: The District of Columbia maintains CON requirements for hospitals only. MedStar Health dominates the constrained urban market with multiple facilities, and prices reach 298% of Medicare rates.

citations.text: Health Services Planning Program Act, D.C. Code § 44-401 et seq.

name: Georgia
marketConcentration: Piedmont Healthcare and Wellstar Health System dominate metro Atlanta. Emory Healthcare controls academic medicine. Three systems control over 60% of inpatient beds.
summary: Georgia's comprehensive CON program covers hospitals, ASCs, and imaging. Piedmont Healthcare and Wellstar dominate the Atlanta market after major consolidation. Active reform efforts have gained momentum since 2022.

name: Hawaii
adminAgency: Hawaii State Health Planning and Development Agency
marketConcentration: The Queen's Health System and Hawaii Pacific Health control the vast majority of inpatient capacity across the island chain. Geographic isolation amplifies market power.
summary: Hawaii's CON program covers all major facility types. Island geography creates natural monopolies amplified by CON restrictions. The Queen's Health System and Hawaii Pacific Health dominate with limited competitive pressure.

name: Illinois
adminAgency: Illinois Health Facilities and Services Review Board
marketConcentration: Advocate Aurora Health (now Advocate Health) and CommonSpirit Health dominate Chicago metro. Northwestern Medicine and Rush University System control academic markets.
summary: Illinois has a comprehensive CON program covering hospitals, ASCs, and imaging, administered by the Health Facilities and Services Review Board. Major health system consolidation in Chicago has created significant market concentration, with four systems controlling majority share.

marketConcentration: UnityPoint Health and MercyOne control majority of inpatient capacity. University of Iowa Hospitals dominates academic care.
dominantSystems: University of Iowa Hospitals and Clinics
summary: Iowa maintains CON requirements only for hospitals. UnityPoint Health and MercyOne dominate the market. Active reform legislation has been introduced in recent sessions.

name: Kentucky
conStatuteTitle: Kentucky Certificate of Need and Licensure Act
adminAgency: Kentucky Cabinet for Health and Family Services
marketConcentration: Three systems — Norton Healthcare, Baptist Health, and CommonSpirit — control 100% of Louisville's inpatient market. Norton alone operates 5 hospitals in metro Louisville with $4.2B in annual revenue.
summary: Kentucky has the most restrictive CON program in the nation, covering hospitals, ASCs, and imaging with no dollar threshold exemptions. Three systems control 100% of Louisville's inpatient market, and prices reach 354% of Medicare rates — the highest in any CON state profiled.

citations.text: Kentucky Certificate of Need and Licensure Act, KRS § 216B.010 et seq.

name: Maine
conStatuteCitation: Me. Rev. Stat. Ann. tit. 22, § 329 et seq.
adminAgency: Maine Department of Health and Human Services
marketConcentration: MaineHealth dominates southern Maine. Northern Light Health controls northern and eastern regions.
summary: Maine maintains CON requirements for hospitals and ASCs. MaineHealth and Northern Light Health dominate a rural market. Detailed market analysis is pending.

name: Maryland
conStatuteCitation: Md. Code Ann., Health-Gen. § 19-120 et seq.
marketConcentration: Johns Hopkins Health System and University of Maryland Medical System dominate. All-payer rate setting creates unique market dynamics alongside CON.
summary: Maryland operates a unique hybrid: CON laws combined with the nation's only all-payer hospital rate-setting system. Johns Hopkins and University of Maryland Medical System dominate the market. Partial reforms have modernized thresholds.

name: Massachusetts
marketConcentration: Mass General Brigham dominates eastern Massachusetts with $18B+ revenue. Beth Israel Lahey Health and UMass Memorial compete for remaining share.
summary: Massachusetts Determination of Need program covers hospitals and ASCs. Mass General Brigham dominates with over $18 billion in revenue, creating one of the most concentrated healthcare markets in the nation. Prices reach 338% of Medicare.

name: Michigan
adminAgency: Michigan Department of Health and Human Services
marketConcentration: Beaumont/Spectrum Health (now Corewell Health) and Trinity Health dominate. Henry Ford Health System controls Detroit market.
summary: Michigan maintains comprehensive CON covering hospitals, ASCs, and imaging. Corewell Health (formed from Beaumont-Spectrum merger) and Trinity Health dominate the market. Active reform efforts continue in the legislature.

name: Minnesota
marketConcentration: Mayo Clinic dominates statewide. Allina Health and Fairview/Sanford control Twin Cities. Hospital moratorium since 1984 freezes new construction.
summary: Minnesota does not have a traditional CON program but maintains a hospital construction moratorium since 1984 that functions similarly. Mayo Clinic dominates, and the moratorium has effectively frozen market entry for four decades.

citations.text: Minnesota Hospital Construction Moratorium, Minn. Stat. § 144.551

Mercatus Center, Certificate-of-Need Laws: Minnesota Profile, 2020
CMS Hospital Cost Report Data, Minnesota Facilities, FY 2023
AHA Annual Survey, Minnesota Hospital Data, 2023

name: Mississippi
marketConcentration: Baptist Memorial Health Care and University of Mississippi Medical Center dominate. Rural hospital closures accelerating despite CON protections.
summary: Mississippi has one of the most restrictive CON programs in the nation, covering hospitals, ASCs, and imaging with low dollar thresholds. Despite claims that CON protects rural hospitals, Mississippi has experienced significant rural hospital closures.

citations.text: Mississippi Certificate of Need Act, Miss. Code Ann. § 41-7-171 et seq.

name: Missouri
marketConcentration: BJC HealthCare and SSM Health dominate St. Louis. HCA Healthcare and Mercy control Kansas City and Springfield. Four systems hold over 70% of inpatient beds.
summary: Missouri requires CON for hospitals and ASCs but not imaging. BJC HealthCare dominates St. Louis with $7.5B+ in revenue. The four largest systems control over 70% of inpatient capacity, and prices reach 292% of Medicare. Active reform efforts have gained bipartisan support.

marketConcentration: Northwell Health ($17B+ revenue), NYU Langone, Mount Sinai, and NewYork-Presbyterian control the metro NYC market. Four systems dominate a $90B+ healthcare economy.
summary: New York has one of the oldest and most comprehensive CON programs in the nation, covering hospitals, ASCs, and imaging. Four mega-systems — Northwell, NYU Langone, Mount Sinai, and NewYork-Presbyterian — control the majority of the $90B+ metro healthcare market.

name: Vermont
marketConcentration: UVM Health Network controls virtually all hospital care in the state. No meaningful competition exists. The Network operates 6 hospitals including UVM Medical Center, the only academic medical center.
summary: Vermont has one of the most restrictive CON programs in the nation, administered by the Green Mountain Care Board. UVM Health Network operates as a near-monopoly, controlling virtually all hospital capacity in the state with no meaningful competition. Prices reach 315% of Medicare rates.

name: West Virginia
conStatuteTitle: West Virginia Health Care Authority Certificate of Need
marketConcentration: WVU Medicine dominates with 20+ hospitals and clinics. CAMC/Vandalia Health controls Charleston market. Two systems hold over 70% of inpatient capacity in a state with severe rural access challenges.
summary: West Virginia maintains comprehensive CON covering hospitals, ASCs, and imaging. WVU Medicine and Vandalia Health (formerly CAMC) control over 70% of inpatient capacity. Despite CON proponents claiming rural protection, the state has experienced significant rural hospital closures and access challenges.
